sentence_reviews.text: Pairwise rankings or comparative judgments teach which outputs or behaviors are preferred.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang et al. RLHF paper, human comparisons/ranking model training; Rafailov et al. DPO paper, preference-pair objective setup; AIMA contents.html, decision/utility and learning chapters.
sentence_reviews.notes: RLHF and DPO support learning from comparative preferences; the sentence generalizes from output rankings to behaviors.

sentence_reviews.text: The role of Preference learning is to connect Supervised learning, Labels, targets, rewards & preferences to downstream capabilities including Margin, ranking & metric objectives, Reward modeling, Preference optimization (RLHF, DPO families).
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang et al. RLHF paper, supervised fine-tuning, reward model from comparisons, and RL optimization stages; Rafailov et al. DPO paper, direct preference optimization from preference pairs; AIMA learning and decision chapters.
sentence_reviews.notes: Reward modeling and DPO/RLHF preference optimization are supported; margin/metric objectives and the full graph bridge are synthesized.

sentence_reviews.text: substrate for Margin, ranking & metric objectives
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: DPO preference-pair objective setup and RLHF comparison ranking/reward-model training.
sentence_reviews.notes: Ranking/preference objectives are supported, but margin and metric objectives are not directly established by the cited evidence.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang et al. RLHF paper, reward model trained from human comparison data.
sentence_reviews.notes: The RLHF source directly supports preference/comparison data as input to reward modeling.

sentence_reviews.text: substrate for Preference optimization (RLHF, DPO families)
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang et al. RLHF paper, human-feedback training pipeline; Rafailov et al. DPO paper, direct optimization using preference data.
sentence_reviews.notes: RLHF and DPO both use preference data, but 'families' and 'substrate for' are atlas synthesis.

sentence_reviews.text: The chosen learning signal and interaction pattern may not match the deployment objective or data-generating process. Its direct dependencies include Supervised learning, Labels, targets, rewards & preferences.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: RLHF and DPO papers on preference/comparison data; AIMA supervised learning and decision/utility chapters.
sentence_reviews.notes: The dependency list is plausible; the generic deployment-objective mismatch limitation is not directly supported by the inspected evidence.

sentence_reviews.text: Preference learning follows the learning setup expressed by Supervised learning.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: RLHF paper supervised fine-tuning and supervised reward-model training from comparisons; DPO supervised preference loss over paired examples; AIMA Chapter 19 supervised learning.
sentence_reviews.notes: Preference learning often uses supervised datasets of comparisons; not every preference-learning method is purely supervised.

sentence_reviews.text: Preference learning uses Labels, targets, rewards & preferences as a reusable ingredient in its defined operation.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: RLHF paper comparison labels/preferences used for reward modeling; DPO paper preference pairs used for the objective.
sentence_reviews.notes: Preferences and rewards are supported; labels/targets are broader but defensible through comparison labels.

sentence_reviews.text: Preference learning uses Game theory & decision theory as a reusable ingredient in its defined operation.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: AIMA contents.html, Chapter 16/17 decision making, utility theory, and value of information; preference-learning packet sources on choosing preferred outputs.
sentence_reviews.notes: Decision theory is relevant to preferences and utility, but game theory is not directly supported for preference learning by the inspected evidence.

edge_reviews.notes: Preference learning commonly trains on labeled comparison examples; trained_under/core is defensible for this atlas node.
Preference labels and rewards are central to RLHF/DPO-style preference learning; uses/core is defensible.
Decision-theoretic utility/preference concepts are relevant, but game theory is not clearly needed. Common strength is plausible but not strongly evidenced.
